name: Autophagy Induction Track Outlook
main_evidence_gaps: The best-controlled 12-month spermidine trial in older adults was null on its primary memory endpoint and secondary outcomes.
Most positive human signals are short-term biomarker or metabolic shifts rather than durable functional aging benefits.
The track overlaps adjacent nutrient-sensing and metabolic interventions, which makes clean attribution to autophagy difficult.
strongest_current_evidence: Short fasting-mimicking diet trials report human changes in autophagic flux markers alongside metabolic movement.
Spermidine interventions appear tolerable in older adults and keep the track clinically visible even though efficacy remains mixed.
interpretation_note: Autophagy induction now deserves a public track baseline because human interventions can point to pathway-level and metabolic movement. But the current evidence remains mixed, short-term, and far from demonstrating durable healthy-lifespan benefit.
